content:
The version of me that lives inside OpenAI’s servers knows things I’ve forgotten. Where I’m traveling next. What I’m building. The way I phrase things when I’m annoyed. As of this month it assembles all of that on its own, in the background, without my asking — and I can’t see the file.
That last part is the one that bothers me.
I build AI products, so I read OpenAI’s “Dreaming V3” announcement on June 4 the way a mechanic listens to an engine. The new memory system synthesizes years of your conversations into a living model of you and updates itself as your life changes — “you’re going to Singapore in July” quietly becomes “you went to Singapore in July” once the trip ends. It’s a genuine leap. And the coverage of the rollout noted, almost in passing, that it also narrows the audit trail: the system decides what to remember about you, and you mostly don’t get to inspect the reasoning.
Every major AI got memory this year. ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini — all of them quietly stopped being strangers. That’s the feature everyone asked for. But somewhere in the rush to make AI remember us, almost nobody asked the question that will matter most in ten years:
